verses.text: A principal defesa de Paulo de sua vida e fé diante de seu próprio povo em Jerusalém ilustra sua flexibilidade como missionário, assim como seu discurso aos filósofos gregos em Atenas havia feito ( **17.22-31** ; veja **1Co 9.20-23** ). Paulo começa reconhecendo sua afinidade com seu povo, explicando seu histórico judaico e treinamento sob o renomado rabino Gamaliel, o Velho, e descrevendo seu desejo zeloso de honrar a Deus em tudo , o que eles compartilhavam ( **At 22.1-3** ). Paulo então descreve sua perseguição aos cristãos ( **22.4-5** ), a revelação de Jesus a ele no caminho para Damasco ( **22.6-10** ), e sua conversão ( **22.11-16** ). Paulo termina seu discurso descrevendo sua conversa com o Senhor no Templo. O Senhor havia predito a rejeição dos judeus à mensagem e enviado Paulo aos gentios ( **22.17-21** ).\n\n

A principal defesa de Paulo de sua vida e fé diante de seu próprio povo em Jerusalém ilustra sua flexibilidade como missionário, assim como seu discurso aos filósofos gregos em Atenas havia feito ( **17.22-31** ; veja **1Co 9.20-23** ). Paulo começa reconhecendo sua afinidade com seu povo, explicando seu histórico judaico e treinamento sob o renomado rabino Gamaliel, o Velho, e descrevendo seu desejo zeloso de honrar a Deus em tudo , o que eles compartilhavam ( **At 22.1-3** ). Paulo então descreve sua perseguição aos cristãos ( **22.4-5** ), a revelação de Jesus a ele no caminho para Damasco ( **22.6-10** ), e sua conversão ( **22.11-16** ). Paulo termina seu discurso descrevendo sua conversa com o Senhor no Templo. O Senhor havia predito a rejeição dos judeus à mensagem e enviado Paulo aos gentios ( **22.17-21** ).\n\n
sob Gamaliel: Veja nota de estudo em 5.34 .

22:4 I persecuted this way - Ταυτην την ὁδον; This doctrine, the way of worshipping God, and arriving at a state of blessedness. See on Acts 9:2 (note). Binding and delivering into prisons - See on Acts 8:3 (note); Acts 9:2 (note).

22:5 The high priest doth bear me witness, etc. - He probably referred to the letters of authority which he had received from the high priest, and the whole estate of the elders, παν το πρεσβυτεριον, the whole of the presbytery, that is, the sanhedrin; and it is likely, that he had those letters to produce. This zeal of his against Christianity was an ample proof of his sincerity as a Pharisaical Jew.

22:6 As I made my journey, etc. - See the whole of this account, and all the particular circumstances, considered at large in the notes on Acts 9:1 (note), etc., and the observations at the conclusion of that chapter.
22:7 And I fell unto the ground,.... And so did those that were with him, Acts 26:14. And heard a voice, saying unto me, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? See Gill on Acts 9:4.

22:8 And I answered, who art thou, Lord?.... See Gill on Acts 9:5. . Acts 22:9 act 22:9 act 22:9 act 22:9And they that were with me saw indeed the light,.... For it shone about them, as well as Saul: and were afraid; the Alexandrian copy, the Vulgate Latin, and Syriac versions, have not this clause; but it stands in the Arabic and Ethiopic versions; the suddenness, greatness, and extraordinariness of the light surprised them, for it was even miraculous: but they heard not the voice of him that spake to me: they heard the voice of Saul, but not the voice of Christ;

22:9 And I said, what shall I do, Lord?.... See Gill on Acts 9:6. . Acts 22:11 act 22:11 act 22:11 act 22:11And when I could not see for the glory of that light,.... Which was above the brightness of the sun, and so dazzled his eyes, that he could not see his way into the city, some of his company took him by the hand, and led him: and being led by the hand of them that were with me, I came unto Damascus but not with the same view he set out with: he took his journey thither, and pursued it, in order to persecute the saints there;

22:10 And one Ananias, a devout man according to the law,.... The Alexandrian copy, and Vulgate Latin version, read only, "a man according to the law"; one whose walk, life, and conversation, were agreeable to it: a strict observer of the law of Moses, both moral and ceremonial: he not only lived a holy life and conversation, according to the moral law, but he religiously and devoutly attended to the rituals of the ceremonial law; and this part of his character the apostle chose to mention, as what would recommend him to the notice of the Jews he now addressed: for though he was a disciple, a believer in Christ, yet as many of the believing Jews did, so he strictly observed the rituals of the law.

22:11 Came unto me,.... Being at the house of Judas, in that street of Damascus called Straight, Acts 9:11 and stood; at the side of him, or by him, putting his hands on him: and said unto me, brother Saul; See Gill on Acts 9:17. receive thy sight, "or look up", and the same hour I looked up upon him; that is, immediately, directly: for so the phrase, "that same hour", is frequently used by the Jews: the words in Num 16:21 "that I may consume them in a moment", are rendered by Onkelos, "that I may consume them in an hour";
Ananias: Veja nota de estudo em 9.17 .\n\n
Ananias: Veja nota de estudo em 9.17 .\n\n
o Justo: Veja também **3.14** ; **7.52** ; **1Jo 2.1** . A justiça era uma das características do Messias (veja **Is 32.1** ; **53.11** ).

22:15 And it came to pass, that when I was come again to Jerusalem,.... Which was three years after his conversion; for he did not immediately return to Jerusalem, but went into Arabia; and when he returned to Damascus, which was three years after he came to Jerusalem; see Gal 1:17 even while I prayed in the temple; the temple was an house of prayer; hither persons resorted for that purpose; and as the apostle had been used to it, he continued this custom, and during the time of prayer he fell into an ecstasy: I was in a trance: and knew not whether he was in the body, or out of the body: whether this was the time he refers to in 2 Cor 12:2 is not certain, though probable.
seja batizado. Tenha seus pecados lavados: Veja a Nota Temática “ Batismo ”.

Enquanto Paulo estava orando no Templo , ele teve uma visão de Jesus dizendo-lhe que o povo de Jerusalém não aceitaria seu testemunho. O Senhor então o enviou aos gentios. A resposta da multidão a este relato ( **22.22-23** ) provou o ponto.\n\n

Enquanto Paulo estava orando no Templo , ele teve uma visão de Jesus dizendo-lhe que o povo de Jerusalém não aceitaria seu testemunho. O Senhor então o enviou aos gentios. A resposta da multidão a este relato ( **22.22-23** ) provou o ponto.\n\n

Enquanto Paulo estava orando no Templo , ele teve uma visão de Jesus dizendo-lhe que o povo de Jerusalém não aceitaria seu testemunho. O Senhor então o enviou aos gentios. A resposta da multidão a este relato ( **22.22-23** ) provou o ponto.\n\n

Os gritos da multidão, jogando fora seus casacos , e lançando punhados de poeira no ar eram provavelmente respostas rituais à blasfêmia percebida. Eles se opuseram e tentaram frustrar as palavras de Paulo que sugeriam a inclusão dos gentios ( **22.21** ). Cf. **Lc 4.16-30** .

22:24 examined by scourging—according to the Roman practice. that he might know wherefore they cried so—Paul's speech being to him in an unknown tongue, he concluded from the horror which it kindled in the vast audience that he must have been guilty of some crime.

Paulo reivindicou seu status de cidadão romano neste momento crítico quando estava prestes a ser torturado para confessar seu suposto crime. A cidadania romana era um ativo valioso, e reivindicá-la falsamente era uma ofensa capital. Seus principais benefícios eram a proibição de flagelação e o direito de apelar ao imperador ( **25.11** ). O comandante ficou assustado por quase ter violado a lei romana (cp. **16.35-39** ).\n\n

Paulo reivindicou seu status de cidadão romano neste momento crítico quando estava prestes a ser torturado para confessar seu suposto crime. isso me custou muito! Durante o início do reinado do Imperador Cláudio (41–54 d.C.), a cidadania romana podia ser comprada, mas era cara.

22:29 After he knew that he was a Roman - He who was going to scourge him durst not proceed to the torture when Paul declared himself to be a Roman. A passage from Cicero, Orat. pro Verr. Acts. ii. lib. v. 64, throws the fullest light on this place: Ille, quisquis erat, quem tu in crucem rapiebas, qui tibi esset ignotus, cum civem se Romanum esse diceret, apud te Praetorem, si non effugium, ne moram quidem mortis mentione atque usurpatione civitatis assequi potuit?

22:30 commanded the chief priests and all their council to appear—that is, the Sanhedrim to be formally convened. Note here the power to order a Sanhedrim to try this case, assumed by the Roman officers and acquiesced in on their part. Next: Acts Chapter 23
